title: RFID Attendance System
subtitle: Tap Your Card — See Your Name!
overview: Build a real RFID attendance system! Students tap an RFID card on a reader, and an LCD display shows their name. Unknown cards display 'Unknown User'. Learn how contactless technology works — the same tech used in metro cards, school ID badges, and tap-to-pay.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — RFID module, LCD wiring, card tap demo, student builds.

keyTopics: RFID = Radio Frequency Identification — uses invisible radio waves, no contact needed
RFID Reader (RC522) — sends a radio signal, card responds with its unique ID (UID)
UID — Every RFID card has a unique number burned in at the factory (like a fingerprint)
SPI Communication — how the RC522 module talks to the Arduino (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS)
LCD 16×2 with I2C — displays 2 lines of 16 characters; I2C needs only 2 wires to Arduino
Attendance Logic — check UID against a list: match = show name, no match = 'Unknown User'
Real-World RFID — Metro cards, school IDs, library cards, UPI tap-to-pay, toll booths
Security Thinking — what happens if someone copies a card? How do real systems stay safe?

codeExamples.title: Step 1 — Read Card UID (Find Your Card's ID First!)
codeExamples.description: Upload this first. Open Serial Monitor at 9600 baud, tap your card, and note down the UID — you'll need it for the attendance code.

codeExamples.title: Step 2 — Full Attendance System (Tap → LCD Shows Name)
codeExamples.description: The complete project! Replace the UID bytes with the ones you found in Step 1. Tap a known card → see name. Tap unknown card → 'Unknown User'.

codeExamples.title: Wiring Reference (Pin Connections)
codeExamples.description: Use these comments as a wiring guide before uploading any code.

activity.title: Build Your Own RFID Attendance Machine
parts: RFID Cards / Key Fobs (at least 2 per group)
USB cable + Laptop with Arduino IDE
steps: Wire the RC522 module to Arduino (use 3.3V — NOT 5V!). See wiring reference above.
Wire the LCD I2C module to Arduino (SDA → A4, SCL → A5, VCC → 5V).
Install libraries: MFRC522 and LiquidCrystal_I2C via Arduino IDE → Library Manager.
Upload Step 1 code, open Serial Monitor, tap your card — write down the UID numbers!
Open Step 2 code, replace the UID bytes with your actual card UIDs and add student names.
Upload Step 2 — tap your card → see your name on LCD! Tap an unregistered card → 'Unknown User'.
Bonus Challenge: Add a buzzer — beep once for known user, two beeps for unknown!

quiz.explanation: RFID = Radio Frequency Identification. It uses radio waves to identify tags/cards without any physical contact — that's why it's called 'contactless'!

quiz.q: What is a UID in an RFID system?
options: The name of the student stored on the card
A Unique ID — a one-of-a-kind number permanently stored in each RFID card
The password to unlock the reader
The radio frequency the card uses
quiz.explanation: Every RFID card has a Unique Identifier (UID) burned in during manufacturing — like a fingerprint. No two cards have the same UID. Our code uses this UID to identify students!

quiz.q: What voltage does the MFRC522 RFID module require?
quiz.explanation: The RC522 module runs on 3.3V. Connecting it to 5V (Arduino's default power pin) will permanently damage it! Always use the 3.3V pin.

quiz.q: In our attendance code, what does the LCD show when an unregistered card is tapped?
quiz.explanation: When the tapped card's UID doesn't match any entry in our student database, the LCD shows 'Unknown User' — access is denied!

quiz.q: What communication protocol does the MFRC522 use to talk to Arduino?
quiz.explanation: RC522 uses SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface) which requires 4 data wires: MOSI, MISO, SCK, and SS. This is faster than I2C for reading card data quickly.

quiz.q: Which protocol does the I2C LCD use to communicate with Arduino?
options: I2C — uses only 2 wires (SDA and SCL)
quiz.explanation: I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) uses just 2 wires: SDA (data) and SCL (clock). On Arduino UNO, SDA = A4 and SCL = A5. The backpack module converts the LCD to I2C!

quiz.q: In the attendance code, where is the student database stored?
options: In the Arduino code as a struct array
quiz.explanation: The database is a struct array inside the Arduino code — each entry holds a UID (byte array) and the student's name. The card only carries its UID; the name mapping lives in the code.

quiz.q: What is the first step before building the full attendance system?
options: Wire the LCD first and display 'Hello World'
Upload Step 1 code to read and find out your card's UID via Serial Monitor
quiz.explanation: You must first discover each card's UID! Upload Step 1, open Serial Monitor, tap each card, and write down the UIDs. Then hardcode those UIDs into Step 2's student database.

quiz.q: Which real-world device uses RFID technology?
options: Mumbai Metro card / Delhi Metro card
quiz.explanation: Metro cards use RFID — tap to enter! Other examples: library cards, school ID badges, UPI tap-to-pay terminals, and toll booth FASTag passes all use RFID.

quiz.q: What does rfid.PICC_HaltA() do in the code?
options: Turns off the RFID reader permanently
Tells the card to stop communicating so the next tap can be read fresh
Saves the card UID to memory
quiz.explanation: PICC_HaltA() tells the card to stop responding. Without it, the reader keeps detecting the same card repeatedly. Calling it allows the next new card tap to be registered properly.

quiz.q: How does the RFID card send its UID to the reader?
options: The reader emits radio waves, which power the card and trigger it to transmit its UID wirelessly
By touching metal contacts on the card
quiz.explanation: RFID cards are passive — they have no battery! The reader sends out radio waves that power the card's tiny antenna, which then transmits its UID back. All wirelessly, no contact needed!

quiz.q: In the student struct, what two pieces of information does each entry store?
quiz.explanation: Each Student struct holds uid[4] — the 4-byte unique identifier — and name (a String). When a card is tapped, the code loops through all entries comparing UIDs to find the matching name.

quiz.q: What Arduino library do we use to control the RFID RC522 module?
quiz.explanation: The MFRC522 library (by miguelbalboa on GitHub) provides all the functions to initialize the RFID reader, detect cards, and read their UIDs. Install it via Arduino IDE → Library Manager.

quiz.q: What is the LCD I2C address used in our code?
quiz.explanation: LiquidCrystal_I2C lcd(0x27, 16, 2) — 0x27 is the I2C address of most common LCD backpack modules. If the LCD shows nothing, the address might be 0x3F instead (run an I2C scanner sketch to find it).

quiz.q: What is a good bonus challenge to add after the basic system works?
options: Replace the Arduino with a calculator
Remove the LCD to save power
Add a buzzer — one beep for known users, two beeps for unknown cards
Print the attendance to paper automatically
quiz.explanation: Adding a buzzer makes the system more like a real access control system! One beep = welcome, two beeps = access denied. Great extension for students who finish early!
